prompt:
We're ripping out the cron library we vendored after last week's incident: it silently mis-handles day-of-month steps, and the only way to test anything built on it is to actually sleep until the schedule fires. I want our own scheduling core — deterministic, clock-injected, so CI can simulate a week of firings in microseconds. Module go-cronsched, package cronsched, this repo, stdlib only.

Layer 1 — expressions. Parse(expr string) (*Schedule, error) for classic 5-field cron: minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week, fields separated by any run of spaces/tabs, leading/trailing whitespace fine. Numeric only — reject JAN/MON style names. Each field takes *, single values, ranges a-b, steps */n and a-b/n, and comma lists mixing all of those (5,10-20/5,45). Bounds: minute 0-59, hour 0-23, dom 1-31, month 1-12, dow 0-7 where BOTH 0 and 7 mean Sunday. Everything else is an error that names the bad field: wrong field count, out-of-range values, reversed ranges (10-5), zero steps, a step on a bare value (5/2 — a step needs * or a range), empty list items, stray dashes, garbage.

On *Schedule:
- Matches(t time.Time) bool — minute resolution; seconds and nanos are ignored.
- Next(after time.Time) time.Time — the first matching minute STRICTLY after `after` (landing exactly on a matching minute returns the following one), seconds/nanos zeroed, computed as wall-clock in after's Location. If nothing matches within 5 years (think 0 0 30 2 *) return the zero Time — and don't discover that by crawling minute-by-minute across years; skip dead months and days whole.
- NextN(after time.Time, n int) []time.Time — the next n fires chained strictly forward; n <= 0 means an empty result, not a panic.
- The two day fields follow the classic cron either/or rule: a day field counts as restricted unless it is written exactly `*` (so */1 IS restricted even though it spans every day). When both dom and dow are restricted, the schedule fires when EITHER matches — 0 0 13 * 5 means Friday OR the 13th. Otherwise it's a plain AND.

Layer 2 — the part I actually care about. NewScheduler(now func() time.Time) *Scheduler; nil means time.Now, tests inject a fake. Entries are named: Add(name, expr string) error (parse failures propagate; empty or duplicate names are errors; a failed Add must not register anything), Remove(name string) bool. Each entry's cursor starts at the clock reading at the moment it was Added. Poll() []Fire with Fire{Name string; At time.Time}: read the clock once, and for every entry return EVERY scheduled instant t with cursor < t <= now — if the daemon was wedged for an hour, a */15 entry owes four fires, not one — then advance the cursors to now. Sort by At, ties by Name. A clock that hasn't advanced (or jumped backwards — ntp happens) yields nothing, never re-delivers, never panics; cursors never move backwards. NextRun(name string) (time.Time, bool) — the next fire strictly after now(), WITHOUT consuming or affecting anything Poll owes; ok=false for unknown names; zero time for never-firing entries. The whole Scheduler must be safe for concurrent use: we poll from one goroutine and add/remove/inspect from others, and every fire must be delivered to exactly one Poll caller — no duplicates, no drops.

Shape I'm after:

  sched := cronsched.NewScheduler(clk.Now)
  _ = sched.Add("backup", "30 3 * * *")
  for _, f := range sched.Poll() { enqueue(f.Name, f.At) }

cronsched_test.go and scheduler_test.go are the acceptance contract — leave them alone. go test -race -timeout 30s ./... must come up green. Split the parsing, the time math, and the scheduler into separate files; a single 400-line cron.go gets bounced in review.
